title: Long-Range Correlated Random Matrices
abstract: Motivated by the importance ascribed to correlations in random matrices used to model phenomena in various scientific disciplines, we report how algebraic correlations between matrix elements affect the eigenvalue statistics and spectral density of random matrices. These correlations, introduced through a long-range correlated percolation model, decay as a power law $\propto r^{-2H}$, with exponent $H > 0$. As $H$ varies, both the eigenvalue distribution and excess kurtosis undergo qualitative changes. At the threshold $H_c = 3/4$, characterized by emergent Gaussian statistics, a sign change in excess kurtosis marks a transition from a fat-tailed generalized $t$-distribution to one that gradually approaches the standard semicircle law for $H \gg H_c$. Our analytical results, based on scaling analysis and supported by extensive numerical simulations, provide clear predictions and uncover novel spectral regimes in random matrix theory. Our results connect techniques from statistical physics, percolation theory, and random matrix analysis, offering a new perspective on universality in correlated ensembles.
summary: This paper explores how power-law correlations in random matrix elements alter their spectral properties. It identifies a phase transition at a critical exponent H = 3/4, where the eigenvalue distribution shifts from heavy-tailed distributions to Gaussian statistics. The authors also uncover a secondary threshold at H = 1/2 that affects spectral rigidity. These findings provide a new framework for understanding universality in non-invariant, spatially embedded random matrix ensembles.
main_problem: Investigating how long-range algebraic correlations between matrix elements affect the eigenvalue statistics and spectral density of random matrices.
main_result: The study identifies a critical threshold at H = 3/4 where the spectral density transitions from heavy-tailed generalized t-distributions to Gaussian statistics, and a rigidity threshold at H = 1/2.
method: Analytical scaling analysis of eigenvalue moments and Wick-type decomposition of the trace of M^4, supported by extensive numerical simulations.
model_or_system: A symmetric random matrix ensemble where elements are generated via a thresholded Gaussian random field on a 2D square lattice with power-law correlations decaying as r^-2H.
key_observables: Spectral density P(lambda), eigenvalue variance, excess kurtosis, and the scaled average of the maximum eigenvalue.
important_parameters: The correlation exponent H, which defines different regimes: 0 <= H <= 1/4 (divergent kurtosis), 1/4 < H < 3/4 (finite positive kurtosis), H = 3/4 (Gaussian transition), and H > 3/4 (standard RMT/semicircle law).
main_assumptions: The underlying Gaussian field is zero-mean, and the nonlinear thresholding process preserves the long-range correlation structure of the original field.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows eigenvalue variance scaling with L; Figure 2 shows excess kurtosis scaling with L; Figure 3 shows the probability density function P(lambda) demonstrating data collapse and agreement with analytical predictions.
paper_structure: The paper moves from the construction of the correlated matrix model to the derivation of scaling laws for moments, identifies specific critical regimes for the exponent H, and concludes with numerical verification of the spectral transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
